af_score_components.notes: Official Argoproj Labs MCP server for Argo CD GitOps management (quality 75). API token via ARGOCD_BASE_URL and ARGOCD_API_TOKEN environment variables. 12 tools: application lifecycle (list, get, create, update, delete, sync) and resource management (resource tree, logs, events, actions). Optional MCP_READ_ONLY mode for safety — strongly recommended for production. Write operations (create, update, delete, sync) directly affect production deployments. Self-signed cert environments require NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 (security risk). Resource actions execute directly on Kubernetes resources — high blast radius.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS for Argo CD connections — enforced by API. Argo CD API token with RBAC — scoped to Argo CD application permissions. Argo CD RBAC provides resource-level permission control (application level, namespace level). NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 for self-signed certs disables TLS verification — security risk in non-isolated environments. Argoproj Labs maintained — official community project. API token via environment variable. Write operations on K8s resources via Argo CD have production-grade blast radius. Use MCP_READ_ONLY for safety.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted Argo CD — no SLA on MCP server. Version 0.5.0 — actively maintained by Argoproj Labs. No formal breaking change policy. No retry guidance documented. Partial idempotency — sync operations may fail silently if Argo CD state is inconsistent. Application management operations have production deployment consequences. Argo CD API stability generally good as part of CNCF ecosystem. Stdio and HTTP stream transports available.

af_score_components.notes: Microsoft's official MCP educational curriculum — 12 modules with hands-on examples in C#, Java, JavaScript, Python, TypeScript, and Rust. MCP example servers exist (quality 78) but designed for learning, not production deployment. Covers basic MCP servers through enterprise Azure integrations with Entra ID, PostgreSQL. Retry guidance documented in some modules. Auth examples covered include Azure Entra ID. 14.5k GitHub stars reflects educational value. Azure-specific modules require Azure subscription and may incur costs. 50+ language translations via GitHub Actions — translated docs may lag behind English. Not a deployable production service.

security_score_components.security_notes: Varies by module — Azure examples enforce HTTPS/Entra ID; local examples may not require TLS. Microsoft maintained — rigorous security review for curriculum. Covers enterprise auth patterns including Entra ID. Tutorial code examples may not be fully production-hardened for security. API key and OAuth examples may demonstrate patterns without comprehensive secret management. Multiple language examples means varying security posture per language module. Educational purpose means code may prioritize clarity over security rigor.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Educational curriculum — module examples are not production services. Microsoft actively maintains and updates curriculum. Retry guidance documented in applicable modules. Azure-specific modules have Azure SLA but require subscription. GitHub-hosted — excellent uptime for documentation access. Code examples may not have comprehensive error handling (educational focus). MCP spec evolution means some examples may become outdated. Not suitable for production deployment as-is.

af_score_components.notes: Plugin-based MCP security gateway from Lasso Security (quality 75). Intercepts and sanitizes MCP requests/responses for PII masking, secret detection, and prompt injection defense. CRITICAL: Gateway itself has NO authentication layer — relies on MCP client as trust boundary. Full security coverage requires commercial Lasso Security API plugin; open-source plugins (Presidio for PII, basic secret patterns) provide limited coverage. Silent redaction of PII/secrets means agents may receive incomplete responses without knowing. Python proxy adds latency hop for every MCP call. Security reputation scanner checks at gateway load time, not continuously. Good enterprise defense-in-depth addition.

security_score_components.security_notes: Self-hosted security gateway. TLS between gateway and downstream MCP servers depends on configuration. CRITICAL: Gateway itself has no authentication — any process that can reach the gateway has access to all MCP servers behind it. This is by design (relies on MCP client auth) but inappropriate for multi-tenant environments. Lasso API key for commercial plugin via environment variable. Presidio for PII masking is well-maintained (Microsoft). Plugin-based architecture limits scope control. Security reputation scanner provides static-time protection only.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted — no SLA. Relatively new project from Lasso Security. No formal breaking change policy. Idempotency: yes — request/response interception is stateless. Silent redaction is a reliability concern — agents may not understand why responses are incomplete. Python proxy adds latency that is not benchmarked. Security reputation scanner operates at load time — no runtime re-scanning. Not suitable for high-throughput production without latency profiling.

af_score_components.notes: Enterprise-grade unified MCP gateway and registry (quality 88). OAuth2 + JWT with fine-grained scopes per MCP server, method, tool, and agent — gold standard for enterprise MCP governance. Multi-provider IAM: Keycloak or Microsoft Entra ID. Human users via web login with JWT; AI agents via OAuth2 Client Credentials. Agent-to-agent (A2A) communication support. Semantic search for autonomous tool discovery. Keycloak setup is non-trivial — most likely failure point during deployment. Each agent identity requires pre-registration. Terraform-based AWS ECS deployment. SOC2/GDPR-ready audit logging. Federated registry with bidirectional sync.

security_score_components.security_notes: Enterprise-grade security posture. HTTPS enforced — AWS/SOC2 ready. OAuth2 + JWT with exceptional scope granularity: per-MCP-server, per-method, per-tool, per-agent scoping. Keycloak or Microsoft Entra ID as IAM provider — industry-standard. SOC2/GDPR-ready audit logging for compliance. Cisco AI Defense integration for security scanning. MongoDB/DocumentDB for persistent state. Agent OAuth2 Client Credentials flow requires pre-registration — intentional security design. Best-in-class security architecture for enterprise MCP deployments.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: AWS ECS deployment with multi-AZ architecture — enterprise reliability posture. SOC2-ready design implies operational maturity. Cursor pagination for large result sets. No retry guidance documented. Keycloak misconfiguration is the most likely production failure point. MongoDB/DocumentDB hybrid search behavior differs between CE and DocumentDB — test in target environment. Federated registry synchronization adds complexity. Version rollback capability documented. Not plug-and-play — significant DevOps investment required.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server for Google Ads (quality 62). Requires Google Ads Developer Token (1-3 business day approval — blocks initial setup). OAuth 2.0 Desktop App credentials or Service Account JSON key. Login Customer ID required for MCC/multi-account setups. All file paths must be absolute — relative paths silently fail. Read-oriented tooling — campaign analysis, GAQL queries, performance metrics. No campaign creation/modification capabilities. Test tokens have undocumented limitations before production approval. Good for ad analysts wanting conversational Google Ads data access.

security_score_components.security_notes: Google Ads API enforces HTTPS. OAuth2 with service account — strong Google auth model. Google Ads Developer Token adds an additional approval layer. Service account has account-level access — limited scope granularity within accounts. Community maintained — dependency hygiene depends on maintainer. OAuth credentials and service account JSON stored as files — absolute path requirement adds configuration complexity. Credential files must have restricted permissions. GAQL read operations only — no write access reduces blast radius.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Ads API has SLA and good uptime. Community MCP server has no formal SLA. No retry guidance documented. Developer Token test mode has undocumented limitations — production behavior differs. Developer Token approval delay (1-3 business days) creates initial setup reliability concern. MCC/multi-account configuration adds complexity. No formal breaking change policy for community MCP. Reliant on Google Ads API version compatibility. Read-only operations provide consistent results.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server for Google Sheets (quality 72). Four auth methods in priority order: base64 env var > service account path > OAuth > Application Default Credentials. Service account requires explicit sharing of target Drive folder. OAuth first-run requires interactive browser login — blocks headless agent flows. All 19 tools loaded by default consuming ~13K tokens; use ENABLED_TOOLS env var to filter. Full CRUD: create, read, update sheets, rows, columns. macOS users must use full uvx path in config. GCP project setup required for all auth methods. Good for AI reporting pipelines writing to shared Sheets.

security_score_components.security_notes: Google Sheets API enforces HTTPS; Google OAuth enforces TLS. Service account or OAuth2 — strong Google auth. Google Sheets API scopes (readonly or full read/write) provide some granularity. Service account limited to shared Drive folders — implicit scope control. Community maintained — dependency hygiene depends on maintainer. Credentials via file paths or base64 env var — base64 encoding is not encryption. OAuth first-run requires interactive browser — headless agent deployment blocked. Service account JSON has full account-level access within shared folders.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Sheets API has SLA. Community MCP server has no formal SLA. No retry guidance documented. Google Sheets API rate limits (read: 300/min/project, write: 60/min/project) require planning for high-frequency operations. OAuth first-run blocking is a reliability concern for headless deployments. 13K token overhead for all 19 tools — use ENABLED_TOOLS in production. Partial idempotency — reads idempotent, writes may create duplicates. No formal breaking change policy for community MCP.

af_score_components.notes: TypeScript MCP server for generic GraphQL API access (quality 65). Auth via HEADERS env var (static JSON, e.g. {"Authorization":"Bearer token"}) — no OAuth flow, no token refresh. Mutations disabled by default via ALLOW_MUTATIONS flag — must explicitly enable for write operations. Schema introspection must be enabled on target GraphQL server (some production APIs disable it). No query complexity or depth limiting — agents could generate expensive queries. Suitable for API key and bearer token scenarios. Good for prototyping and internal GraphQL API access. Not suitable for APIs requiring dynamic token refresh.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS depends on target GraphQL endpoint. Static HEADERS env var for auth — no token refresh means tokens must be manually rotated when they expire. No query complexity limiting — agents can generate expensive or resource-exhausting queries. No MCP-level scope control — access reflects whatever the static auth token provides. Community maintained TypeScript project. Auth tokens in HEADERS env var — not logged but must be protected. Mutations disabled by default is a good security default. Schema introspection may leak sensitive type information.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted — no SLA. Depends on target GraphQL endpoint uptime and stability. Community maintained. No formal breaking change policy. No retry guidance documented. Static auth token expiry requires manual rotation — creates operational reliability concern. Schema introspection must be enabled on target API — not guaranteed. No query complexity limits mean long-running queries may timeout MCP clients. Partial idempotency — queries are idempotent, mutations are not.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server for Google Search Console (quality 65). OAuth (client_secrets.json from GCP Console) or service account (JSON key file). Service account email must be manually added to each GSC property as a user — not automatic. GSC data has 2-3 day delay — agents report stale data for recent content. Domain verification must exist in GSC before auth access works. Visualizations described in docs rely on Claude's interpretation, not native chart rendering. Read-oriented: search analytics, URL indexing status, property management, sitemap operations. GCP project and Search Console API must be enabled first.

security_score_components.security_notes: Google Search Console API enforces HTTPS. OAuth2 or service account — strong Google auth. Search Console API scopes (readonly or full) provide scope control. Service account access must be explicitly granted per-GSC-property — implicit per-property scoping. Community maintained Python project. Credentials via JSON file — file permissions must be restricted. OAuth client secrets in JSON file require protection. Service account per-property grant process adds manual administration overhead. GSC data is SEO intelligence — competitive sensitivity.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Search Console API has SLA. Community MCP server has no formal SLA. No retry guidance documented. GSC data latency (2-3 days) is a structural reliability limitation — not a bug but affects freshness expectations. Property verification and service account per-property grants must be maintained. No formal breaking change policy for community MCP. Google API quota limits affect high-frequency querying. Read operations are idempotent. OAuth token refresh required for long-running agents.

af_score_components.notes: DEPRECATED: JetBrains MCP proxy server — functionality built into JetBrains IDEs 2025.2+. New users should use the built-in IDE MCP server instead. No auth required — communicates locally via 127.0.0.1 by default. External connections require explicit 'Can accept external connections' IDE setting. Node.js 18+ required. macOS/nvm users must create symlink in /usr/local/bin for node discoverability. IDE must be open and running before restarting Claude Desktop. Useful only for JetBrains IDE versions prior to 2025.2 or Docker container IDE setups. Do not use for new deployments — migrate to built-in MCP.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local-only by default (127.0.0.1) — implicit network isolation boundary. No authentication mechanism — any local process can connect. Enabling external connections opens to network access without auth — significant risk. No scope control. JetBrains officially maintained despite deprecation. No credentials stored. 127.0.0.1 default binding provides adequate isolation for single-user local dev. Deprecated status means security updates may slow. Node.js 18+ dependency must be kept updated.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: DEPRECATED — JetBrains provides a clear migration path to built-in MCP in IDE 2025.2+. IDE must be open and running — reliability dependent on IDE uptime. Official JetBrains maintenance continues for legacy support. No retry guidance. Unknown idempotency. Node 18+ version requirement must be maintained. External connection mode requires IDE setting awareness. 404 errors if 'Can accept external connections' not enabled. Prefer built-in IDE MCP for all new setups.

af_score_components.notes: Flask API bridge for AI-assisted Kali Linux command execution (quality 48). Designed for ISOLATED, AUTHORIZED penetration testing and CTF challenges ONLY. No authentication by design — intended for single-user dedicated Kali VM. Any process reaching port 5000 has full arbitrary shell execution. No command timeout enforcement — long-running tools (nikto, full nmap scans, metasploit) block indefinitely. No command filtering or allowlist — destructive commands execute without warning. Output truncation on large tool outputs. Binding to 0.0.0.0 creates unauthenticated RCE exposure. Excellent for authorized CTF and pentest workflows in properly isolated labs.

security_score_components.security_notes: CRITICAL SECURITY: No authentication whatsoever — any process reaching port 5000 has full arbitrary shell execution on the Kali host, often as root. No TLS — HTTP only on port 5000. No scope control, no command filtering, no allowlisting. No audit logging for compliance. Flask-based minimal implementation. By design for isolated single-user lab use only. NEVER bind to 0.0.0.0 without VPN or strict firewall. NEVER use outside a dedicated personal Kali VM. Security score of 18 in existing evaluation reflects the complete absence of security controls — this is accurate and intentional for the target use case.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted local lab tool — no SLA. Community maintained. No formal breaking change policy. No command timeout enforcement — blocking on long-running tools is a reliability concern. Partial idempotency — read operations (nmap scans) idempotent; write/exploit operations are not. Very large tool outputs may exceed MCP context windows. No error recovery for failed commands. Intended for interactive human-supervised lab sessions, not unattended automation. Kali VM must be running and accessible.
